critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design with genuine attention to leakage, temporal ordering, and confound control. I'll work through the real issues I can identify, organized by root cause.

---

**Issue Group 1: The Exposure Set (Candidate Pool) Encodes the Prior Ranker's Beliefs**

The design limits training labels to the top-20 products returned by the *current* (BM25-based) ranker. This means the LTR model is trained exclusively on products the prior ranker deemed worth showing. For long-tail or ambiguous queries — the specific case the hypothesis targets — the prior ranker is most likely to have a poor candidate set, because BM25 will fail to retrieve semantically relevant but lexically distant products. The LTR model therefore learns a re-ranking function over an already-filtered set, not a retrieval function over the full product catalog.

The practical consequence: on long-tail queries, the *relevant* product may never appear in the top-20 candidate pool, so it will never receive a positive label, never appear in training, and the LTR model will have no signal that it should be ranked higher. NDCG@10 at evaluation time is computed over the same candidate pool (per section 6: 'evaluated on the same candidate pools'), so the metric cannot detect this failure mode — it only measures how well the LTR model re-ranks what BM25 already retrieved.

Claim being made: Restricting labels to the top-20 prevents position bias without harming label quality for the targeted query types.
Why it might be wrong: BM25 systematically under-retrieves for long-tail/ambiguous queries, so the candidates available for re-ranking are precisely the cases where candidate pool quality is lowest. The LTR model may achieve similar or better NDCG@10 on these queries not by finding better matches but by re-ordering a uniformly poor candidate set. The hypothesis ('LTR outperforms BM25 on long-tail queries') would appear confirmed while the actual mechanism (better semantic matching) is never tested.
Evidence: Compute the fraction of purchased products that appear in the top-20 candidate pool, stratified by query tail-ness. If long-tail queries have substantially lower purchase-product recall in the pool, the experiment cannot test the hypothesis as stated. A retrieval recall@20 audit by query frequency quartile would reveal this.

---

**Issue Group 2: Interaction Features for Long-Tail Queries Are Sparse to the Point of Being Uninformative**

Two of the four interaction features are query-product pair-specific: 'Frequency of clicks on (query, product) pair in training' and 'Purchase conversion rate of (query, product) pair in training.' Long-tail queries, by definition, appear rarely in the training data. For a query appearing once or twice in 5 months, these pair-level statistics will be zero or undefined for virtually all candidate products, falling back to a global prior or zero-imputation. The features that are supposed to carry signal for the targeted cases will have the least signal precisely there.

This is not fatal on its own — the embedding cosine similarity feature can carry signal for long-tail queries without pair-level history — but it means the model's advantage on long-tail queries must come almost entirely from the embedding feature and product-level features, not from the novel interaction signals the hypothesis is about. The hypothesis claims interaction signals are the mechanism; the design doesn't cleanly test that mechanism for its own target population.

Claim: Interaction signals improve long-tail ranking.
Why it might be wrong: Pair-level interaction features have near-zero coverage on long-tail queries. Any improvement on long-tail queries comes from the frozen pre-trained embedding (which is not a contribution of this experiment) or from product popularity features (which is the confound the design tries to control). The ablation of 'model without product-level features' doesn't isolate this — it removes popularity features, not interaction features.
Evidence: Report feature importance and feature coverage (fraction of (query, product) pairs with non-zero values) stratified by query frequency quartile. If pair-level interaction features have <10% coverage on bottom-quartile queries, the interaction-signal hypothesis cannot be confirmed for that population.

---

**Issue Group 3: IPW Estimation Depends on the Same Ranker Whose Bias It's Trying to Correct**

Inverse propensity weights are computed from 'the original ranker's position-wise click rates.' This is a global position-wise CTR curve. The standard problem with this approach: it assumes position bias is the *only* source of differential click probability across positions — that is, it assumes the ranker places items in positions independently of their relevance. In practice, the BM25 ranker places more relevant items higher. The observed position-wise CTR therefore conflates position bias with relevance-driven placement. The IPW correction will over-correct: it will up-weight clicks at low positions (which receive fewer clicks partly because they're less relevant, not just because they're lower) and under-weight clicks at high positions.

For the specific hypothesis, this matters because long-tail queries are where the ranker is most uncertain and most likely to misplace items, making the conflation most severe.

Claim: IPW based on position-wise CTR removes position bias.
Why it might be wrong: Position-wise CTR estimates are confounded by relevance ordering. True propensity estimation requires a randomization experiment (e.g., result swapping or random ranking) that the design does not mention. Without this, the IPW correction introduces systematic error whose direction and magnitude are unknown.
Evidence: The design mentions 'comparing validation NDCG@10 with IPW on vs. off' — this is a weak test because both estimates are noisy and confounded. A proper test would require an offline evaluation on a held-out set of queries where positions were randomized (even a small randomization bucket from production would suffice). If no such randomization data exists, the IPW correction should be presented as an approximation with unknown bias, not as a validated debiasing step.

---

**Issue Group 4: Stratification by Query Category Violates Temporal Ordering**

Section 4 states the temporal split is respected (Jan–May train, Jun–Jul val, Aug–Sep test), but also that 'we stratify by query category to ensure each set contains a representative mix.' These two requirements are in tension. If stratification is applied post-hoc within each time window, it's fine. But the description is ambiguous — 'stratify by query category' in the context of a temporal split could mean resampling or filtering queries so that category distributions are balanced, which would require pulling queries from future time windows into earlier splits or discarding queries, disrupting the temporal ordering.

This is a lesser concern than the above if stratification is applied as a filter (only ensuring each window has examples of each type, not rebalancing proportions), but the design should be explicit. If query category distribution genuinely shifts over the 9-month period (e.g., seasonal categories), forced stratification could artificially smooth a real distribution shift and make the test set easier than production.

Claim: Stratification ensures representativeness without violating temporal ordering.
Why it might be wrong: If the category distribution is non-stationary (likely in e-commerce — seasonal goods shift), enforcing uniform category mix in the test window removes a natural source of distribution shift that the model must handle in production. The test NDCG would be measured on an artificially stabilized distribution, inflating estimates of robustness.
Evidence: Report the empirical query category distribution by month. If categories are stable, stratification is harmless. If there is meaningful seasonal drift, the unstratified temporal test is a better proxy for production performance.

---

**Summary:**

The design is above average for this class of experiment. The temporal split, metadata alignment, and single-pass test evaluation are all correct. The most fundamental flaw is Issue 1: the candidate pool is controlled by the very ranker the LTR model is supposed to outperform, and the evaluation shares that same pool — making it structurally impossible to detect failure on the claimed target population (long-tail/ambiguous queries where BM25 retrieves poorly). Issue 2 compounds this: the interaction features have minimal coverage precisely on long-tail queries. Issue 3 (IPW bias) is a known approximation that the design presents more confidently than it deserves. Together, Issues 1 and 2 mean the hypothesis as stated — 'interaction signals improve ranking on long-tail queries' — cannot be confirmed or disconfirmed by this experiment as designed.
all_issues_raised: The candidate pool for both training and evaluation is controlled by the BM25 ranker, so the LTR model is only re-ranking what BM25 retrieved; on long-tail queries where BM25 retrieves poorly, relevant products may never appear in the pool, making the hypothesis untestable for its primary target population.

Pair-level interaction features (query-product click frequency, conversion rate) have near-zero coverage on long-tail queries by definition, so any LTR improvement on long-tail queries must come from the pre-trained embedding or product popularity features, not from the interaction signals the hypothesis attributes as the mechanism.

Inverse propensity weights are estimated from position-wise CTR on the same ranker whose relevance-driven ordering confounds the position bias signal, making the IPW correction an approximation with unknown systematic bias rather than a validated debiasing step.

Query category stratification within a temporal split may smooth real seasonal distribution shifts, producing an artificially stable test distribution that overestimates robustness to the production distribution.

Pair-level interaction features (query-product click frequency, conversion rate) have near-zero coverage on long-tail queries by definition, so any LTR improvement on long-tail queries must come from the pre-trained embedding or product popularity features, not from the interaction signals the hypothesis attributes as the mechanism.

Inverse propensity weights are estimated from position-wise CTR on the same ranker whose relevance-driven ordering confounds the position bias signal, making the IPW correction an approximation with unknown systematic bias rather than a validated debiasing step.

Query category stratification within a temporal split may smooth real seasonal distribution shifts, producing an artificially stable test distribution that overestimates robustness to the production distribution.
